checks.a_analysis: I have reviewed the output JSON against the source PDF. All text elements in the JSON, including the headings, meeting details, agenda items, and contact information, have a direct basis in the source document. No fabricated paragraphs, sentences, or structural elements like table-of-contents or introductions were found. The metadata title and table descriptors are consistent with the list of allowed AI-generated additions.

The source document contains one URL: 'http://it.nc.gov/gicc'. This URL is accurately captured in the JSON output. No other URLs, phone numbers, or email addresses were found in either the source or the output.

All content from the single-page source PDF is present in the JSON. This includes the header logo, the organization name, meeting details, the complete 11-item agenda table, the future meeting date section, and the website information.

The source document contains a table with a header row and 11 data rows corresponding to the agenda items. The JSON representation of the table includes exactly 1 header row and 11 data rows, successfully capturing all items including the multi-line content in item 8.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: The content within each section is fully represented. The complex agenda item 8 (Committee Reports) has been captured with its sub-bullets and corresponding member names intact within the table structure.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text in the output faithfully represents the wording of the source. Minor formatting adjustments, such as combining the top lines 'North Carolina' and 'GEOGRAPHIC INFORMATION COORDINATING COUNCIL' into a single heading, are acceptable reformatting. No changes were found that alter the meaning of the information.

checks.a_analysis: I have reviewed the entire document and compared it with the source PDF. There are no instances of duplicated paragraphs, sections, or list items. Each content block from the source appears exactly once in the converted output.

The document uses H2 for the primary title and the main 'AGENDA' heading. While it skips H1, this is a form of level adjustment/normalization common for accessibility in segmented outputs. There are no skips between the existing levels (e.g., H2 to H4), and the hierarchy is flat but logical.

The table structure accurately reflects the 3-column layout of the source document (TIME, AGENDA ITEM, GICC MEMBER or GUEST). Rows are correctly identified, including header rows. Complex content in the 'Committee Reports' row is maintained within a single table row, preserving the grid integrity.

The output follows the vertical reading order of the source PDF, starting with the header information, then the main table, and finally the footer meeting dates and website URL. This matches the intended flow of information.

In the table row for 'Agenda Item 8', the source document contains a nested list with bullets and sub-bullets. In the output JSON, this entire list is merged into a single text block within the table cell, using newline characters and manually typed bullet markers (•, o) instead of a proper semantic list structure. This merging of list items into a single paragraph block is a structural failure.
checks.b_evidence_source:
8. Committee Reports
• Statewide Mapping Advisory Committee
 o Vote: Standard for Parcel Data Content (Summary)
• Local Government
 o Vote: LGC Bylaws Revised...
checks.b_evidence_output:
"text": "8. Committee Reports
• Statewide Mapping Advisory Committee
  o Vote: Standard for Parcel Data Content (Summary)
• Local Government
  o Vote: LGC Bylaws Revised..."

checks.a_analysis: The source document contains several visible hyperlinks that were missed in the conversion. Specifically, 'Minutes' in Item 3, 'Standard for Parcel Data Content (Summary)' in Item 8, and 'LGC Bylaws Revised' in Item 8 are all blue and underlined in the PDF, indicating they are links. These appear only as plain text in the output. Only the URL at the bottom of the document was correctly converted as a link.
checks.b_evidence_source: Items in blue/underlined text: 'Minutes' (Item 3), 'Standard for Parcel Data Content (Summary)' (Item 8), 'LGC Bylaws Revised' (Item 8).
checks.b_evidence_output: Plain text is used for these items without accompanying href or link objects.
checks.b_evidence_location: Section 1, Table, row index 3 and row index 8

checks.a_analysis: The overall visual organization of the document is preserved. The header information, logo, and agenda table are presented in a logical order that mirrors the source PDF. While there are minor differences in font and spacing, the user can find the same information in roughly the same place.

The table rendering for agenda item 8 ('Committee Reports') is visually broken. In the source PDF, the sub-committees are listed with their respective representatives horizontally aligned on the same line (e.g., 'Statewide Mapping Advisory Committee' is aligned with 'Ryan Draughn'). In the HTML output, all sub-committees are grouped into a single cell under 'AGENDA ITEM' and all representatives are grouped into a single cell under 'GICC MEMBER or GUEST'. This destroys the visual alignment and mapping between committees and people, making the table data difficult to parse and understand correctly.
checks.b_evidence_source: Item 8 shows sub-bullets (e.g., 'Statewide Mapping Advisory Committee', 'Local Government') horizontally aligned with specific names on the right (e.g., 'Ryan Draughn', 'Kathryn Clifton').
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML table row for item 8 collapses all sub-bullets into one cell and all names into another, losing the row-level correspondence. For example, 'Ryan Draughn Kathryn Clifton John Farley...' appears as a single block of text in the right-hand cell.

checks.a_analysis: The NCGICC logo present in the source PDF header is correctly rendered in the HTML output.
No visual content duplication was observed in the rendered HTML output. Each section of the agenda and the meeting details appear only once.
